author: @mattthesparky
text: Hi Robert, do you know if the graphene you are producing with your liquid phase exfoliation methods here is single layer graphene or few layer graphene? I am planning an experiment making a graphene/activated carbon/thermal copolymer powder for use as toner in my laser printer. I have already had some success printing activated carbon straight to aluminum foil in this thing and I was hoping to add some graphene for conductivity. it's pretty cheap to buy the few layer stuff but very expensive to buy single layer so I was just wondering what level of exfoliation you actually get with this method. cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Mattthesparky you can get single layer mate - this was based on the work at Trinity, Dublin - check out their articles

replies.author: @mattthesparky
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith  good one, thank very much for your reply. it is great that you continue to help others who are experimenting. It is not often that when someone such as yourself gets to prototype stage with their product that they continue to share their knowledge. thanks again and best of luck with your exceptional printed capacitors. I hope to be installing them in my mobile solar PV units one day.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Mattthesparky I am very keen on sharing the knowledge when i can mate

author: @임지호-r3m
text:
Hello I'm JIHO LIM from South Korea.
I am studying in KyungHee university majoring in Advanced material engineering as junior.
As our team is doing a project, making supercapacitor with graphene, the first step is to synthesize the Graphene oxide.
For the first step in synthesizing the graphene oxide, we use your youtube video as reference and put mixture of 360ml 96% Sulphuric acid with 40ml 75% Phosphoric acid into the 3g graphite plus 18g Potassium permanganate.
As we do the experiment putting the acid solution, there are many graphite scattering. And as it is acid solution, it's very dangerous and take long times. But on your video, you put it very easily. I followed your procedures but there's difference. Can you tell how to do this step in detail?
My email is superherojiho@gmail.com
And as I'm doing this experiment, I'd like to keep contact you if you don't mind.
Thank you. I'm looking forward your reply.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: 임지호 i won't write a detailed recipe - the time involved is too great - rewatch the video - it is a simple process

author: @podgypig120
text: Hi RMS, great videos. Can you elaborate why this is better than pure acetone. Looks like type 10 contains acetone..? and should the volumes be the same?(75/25%)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: hiya mate - this was just a demonstration of a common chemical available all over the world that forms dispersions of graphene quite easily -it is superior only really in respect of it does a good job and is easy to get hold of - very often with clinical reagents you need a lab license to hold them - with this you don't. Pure acetone i actually quite poor for forming graphene dispersions and only really works as part of a co-solvent system. You wouldn't mix your own - you would just buy the type 10 and use it as is - that's why the video. For more on the reasons why this works you need to read up on Hansen solubility parameters - there are some good posts in the other vids on solvents pointing to where to get the info

replies.author: @podgypig120
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Thanks for the response. I've watched all your vids,  shall carry on reading the oceans of info out there!
